title: Why I Wear Mostly Black
subtitle: A personal philosophy on colour, simplicity, and what matters
author: Archive Curator
content:
I stopped thinking about colour five years ago and it simplified everything.

Black Supima tees, black Massimo Dutti trousers, black leather belt. This combination is my default. It's not a limitation—it's the opposite. I can focus on what actually matters: proportion, fit, material texture, how light catches the wool. Colour isn't competing for attention.

Wearing black means I can't rely on visual novelty. My Samba OG white sneakers have to be *really* clean. My Cartier watch has to sit perfectly. My posture has to be correct. A black outfit exposes every failure in fit because there's nothing else to look at.

Practically: everything coordinates. I don't think about whether my shirt matches my trousers. I don't need multiple belts or different shoes for different contexts. My morning decisions evaporate. Black simplifies until only quality remains.

The philosophy: when I remove colour, people see me instead of my clothes. My presence. My proportions. Whether I'm standing up straight. This matters more than the garment itself.

Some people say monochrome is boring. They're right—and that's the point. Boring is honest. Boring doesn't distract. Boring reveals what you actually value: craftsmanship, time invested, material quality.
